ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general baguette entry (FOODON_03301710); no specific Vietnamese baguette (banh mi) entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers bread and rolls as a category; no banh mi-specific code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559003 is the closest parent for bread as a substance; no banh mi-specific code exists.

main_ingredients: rice flour (sometimes added for lighter crust)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard banh mi roll (plain bread, no filling) weighing approximately 80 g, as typically served at Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bread, French or Vienna, unenriched (USDA SR Legacy, NDB No. 18033)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Banh mi bread is a Vietnamese adaptation of the French baguette, made with wheat flour and sometimes a small proportion of rice flour, producing a lighter, crispier crust. USDA SR Legacy French/Vienna bread (NDB 18033) is the closest approved analogue for the plain bread component. Values are used as-is for the plain bread shell; fillings are excluded. No Singapore HPB Foodo entry exists for plain banh mi bread as a standalone item.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — baguette / white bread analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for banh mi bread specifically. Banh mi is structurally and compositionally analogous to a French baguette (white wheat flour, low fat, low fibre, high starch). The University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table (Am J Clin Nutr 2008;87:1-20) report GI of 75 (glucose=100) for white baguette-style bread. This value is applied as the closest defensible analogous estimate. GL per serving = (75 × 41.6 g carbohydrate per 80 g serving) / 100 = 31.2.

hypertension.guidance: Plain banh mi bread contains approximately 536 mg sodium per 100 g, which is moderate-to-high; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid high-sodium fillings such as processed meats or pickled vegetables when consuming banh mi.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index and high refined carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Banh mi bread has a high GI of approximately 75 and delivers around 41.6 g of rapidly digestible carbohydrate per standard 80 g roll, making it likely to cause a significant postprandial blood glucose rise; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size, pair with protein and fibre-rich fillings, and monitor blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat and zero cholesterol in plain bread
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain banh mi bread is low in saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, posing minimal direct cardiovascular risk from the bread itself; however, the sodium content warrants attention, and cardiovascular risk is primarily determined by the fillings chosen.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Banh mi (Bánh mì) is a Vietnamese-style baguette that emerged from French colonial influence in Vietnam during the 19th century. The plain bread shell is made primarily from wheat flour, water, yeast, and salt, sometimes with a small proportion of rice flour added to produce a characteristically thin, shatteringly crisp crust and a light, airy crumb — distinguishing it from a standard French baguette. In Singapore, banh mi is served at Vietnamese restaurants and food stalls, most commonly as a filled sandwich with ingredients such as pâté, cold cuts, pickled daikon and carrot, cucumber, coriander, and chilli. This record covers the plain bread component only, without fillings. Nutritionally, the plain bread is a high-carbohydrate, moderate-protein, low-fat food with a high glycemic index (approximately 75), making it a significant source of rapidly available glucose. It provides useful amounts of B vitamins (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, folate) and iron from the wheat flour. Sodium content is moderate-to-high at approximately 536 mg per 100 g. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the high GI and carbohydrate load require careful portion management. The bread is naturally vegan, dairy-free, and halal-suitable, but contains gluten and is not suitable for those with coeliac disease or wheat allergy.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a banh mi roll?
Is banh mi bread high GI?
Is banh mi suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of banh mi bread?
Is banh mi bread gluten free?
What is the difference between banh mi and a French baguette?
How much carbohydrate is in a banh mi roll?

confidence_reason: No HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for plain banh mi bread as a standalone food. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA SR Legacy French/Vienna bread entry (NDB 18033), which is the closest approved analogue. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database baguette/white bread data. Confidence is medium because the match is analogous rather than exact.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy French/Vienna bread (NDB 18033) as the closest approved analogue for plain banh mi bread. The Vietnamese banh mi bread is compositionally similar but may include a small proportion of rice flour, which slightly lowers protein and gluten content; this difference is considered minor for nutritional purposes at the per-100g level. 2. GI of 75 is an analogous estimate based on white baguette-style bread from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI table. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy source for this bread type. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based bread with no animal-derived fat. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as bread contains no vitamin C. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for unenriched French bread. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is an unenriched plant-based bread. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for this bread type. 4. This record covers the plain bread shell only; fillings (pâté, cold cuts, pickled vegetables, etc.) are excluded. 5. The cuisine field is set to 'Vietnamese' as banh mi is a Vietnamese food item, though it is served in Singapore. The input record listed cuisine as 'Singapore', which reflects the serving context rather than the food's origin; this has been corrected to 'Vietnamese' per canonical food identity rules. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all reported from USDA SR Legacy NDB 18033 and are not sentinel values.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected cuisine from 'Singapore' (serving context) to 'Vietnamese' (food origin) per canonical food identity rules.
Added Vietnamese local name 'Bánh mì' as primary local name.
Added Singaporean multilingual names (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) per G6 requirements for Singapore-served dishes.
Populated all nutrient values from USDA SR Legacy NDB 18033 (French/Vienna bread) as closest approved analogue.
Set GI=75 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 for white baguette-style bread.
Documented all zero values (trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b12_mcg, vitamin_d_mcg) with 'confirmed zero' justification in notes.
Ensured all aliases_by_language entries appear verbatim in flat aliases list.
Used only allowed match_type values: exact, closest_parent, none.
Included specific clinical guidance for all three required health conditions including Type 2 Diabetes.
